Raytheon-Rafael Joint Venture Gets $1.25 Billion Missile Contract With Israel
data.description:
By Kelly Cloonan

A joint venture between RTX's Raytheon and Rafael Advanced Defense Systems notched a $1.25 billion contract to supply Israel with missiles and related equipment.

Under the contract, the companies will use a new factory in Arkansas to support the Israel Missile Defense Organization's aims to boost production of Iron Dome interceptors, providing missiles, missile kits and test equipment, they said Friday.

The joint venture's Iron Dome Weapon System uses missiles to intercept shorter-range targets like rockets, artillery and mortars to defend populated areas, according to Raytheon's website.

The companies said they used a $33 million capital investment to establish the new factory, which opened earlier this month. The site will produce missiles for the Iron Dome Weapon System and for the Marine Corps' Medium-Range Intercept Capability program.


Write to Kelly Cloonan at kelly.cloonan@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

11-21-25 1144ET

October, November Inflation Data to Get Delayed December Release
data.description:
By Matt Grossman


The federal government won't release a standalone inflation report for October. It will instead publish limited October price numbers together with November data with a delayed release on Dec. 18, the Bureau of Labor Statistics said Friday.

The announcement rounds out the BLS's plans for catching up on major statistical reports that were delayed by the record-setting government shutdown. It also means that the Federal Reserve will have jobs and inflation data no more recent than September's on hand when officials meet to make their final planned interest-rate decision of 2025 on Dec. 9-10.

Earlier this week, the agency said that it won't publish an October jobs report either, instead planning to release last month's payroll numbers and November data together next month.

Speculation about how the BLS would handle last month's figures has preoccupied economists and policymakers in recent weeks. White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt had said last week that the October jobs and inflation reports may never be released.

With government data workers off the job last month, key surveys that measure consumer prices and employment status didn't happen.

That meant an unprecedented challenge for the BLS, already under pressure from budget shortfalls and from President Trump's firing of its commissioner, Erika McEntarfer, over the summer. A career official, William Wiatrowski, is now leading the agency as acting commissioner.

The delayed data will add to the challenge facing Fed officials who are divided over whether to make their third interest-rate cut of the year in December. The central bank's policymakers are split on whether persistent inflation is the most pressing concern--warranting a choice to hold rates steady--or whether a cooling labor market means that rates should be cut further to cushion a slowing economy.

Friday morning, New York Fed President John Williams suggested he could support another rate cut in the near term. After those comments from Williams, a close ally of Fed Chair Jerome Powell, traders leaned more heavily into bets that a December cut is on the way.


Write to Matt Grossman at matt.grossman@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

11-21-25 1141ET

data.title: Harbour Energy Explores U.S. Deals -- Commodities -2-
data.description:
0839 GMT - Oil prices extend losses in early trading after Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky agreed to work on a U.S.-drafted peace plan, easing geopolitical concerns. Brent crude is down 1.2% at $62.62 a barrel, while WTI is down 1.4% at $58.15 a barrel. Both benchmarks are headed for a weekly loss of around 3% as prospects of an oversupplied market continue to weigh on sentiment. Meanwhile, market watchers continue to closely monitor Russian flows as U.S. sanctions on Russia's Rosneft and Lukoil come into effect. "Any peace deal could have broader implications for the oil market," analysts at ANZ say. "If accompanied by the elimination of U.S. sanctions on Russian oil, it could release more oil onto the global market." (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

--

Iron Ore Prices Fall Amid High Inventory, Lower Demand -- Market Talk

0235 GMT - Iron ore prices are lower in early Asia trade, weighed by high inventory and lower demand. The demand has been weak recently given the lack of profitability of steel mills, Baocheng Futures analysts write in a note. The inventory remains higher and overseas shipments have been strong as well, both weighing on prices, they add. The most actively traded January iron-ore contract on the Dalian Commodity Exchange is 0.7% lower at CNY782.5 a ton.(jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

--

Copper Falls Amid Uncertainty Over Fed Path -- Market Talk

0215 GMT - Copper prices fall in Asian trade, with the three-month copper contract on the LME down 0.3% at $10,710.00. The delayed September U.S. nonfarm payrolls report has led to more uncertainty over the Fed's December decision. "For those in favor of a pause in December, the report did not deliver evidence of a rapidly deteriorating labor market," ANZ Research analysts say in a note. But the data wasn't strong either. Meanwhile, China's housing market remains weak, denting construction activity and weighing on metals demand, ANZ adds. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)


Write to Barcelona Editors at barcelonaeditors@dowjones.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

11-21-25 1140ET

data.title: Kansas City Fed Services Activity Index Falls in November
data.description:
By Colin Kellaher


Services activity in the middle of the U.S. fell again in November and expectations for future activity moved lower, according to a monthly survey by the Federal Reserve Bank of Kansas City released Friday.

The Tenth District Services Survey's composite index, a weighted average of indexes covering revenue/sales, employment and inventory, came in at minus 7 in November following readings of minus 5 in October and minus 9 in September. Readings above zero indicate expansion, while those below zero indicate contraction.

The Kansas City Fed said activity in the auto-services and tourism sectors fell in November, while information-services activity rose slightly.

Regional employment fell to its lowest level since April 2020, with further declines expected, the survey found.

The bank said its index of expectations for future services activity moved into negative territory, falling to minus 1 in November from 3 in October.

The Kansas City Fed's survey includes participants from such service industries as retail and wholesale trade, automobile dealers, real estate and restaurants. The survey provides information on current services activity in the Tenth District, which includes Colorado, Oklahoma, Wyoming, Nebraska, Kansas, the northern half of New Mexico and the western third of Missouri.

The bank's monthly manufacturing survey, released Thursday, found that activity at factories in the central U.S. accelerated in November, helped by improved employment and shipments.


Write to Colin Kellaher at colin.kellaher@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

11-21-25 1140ET

data.title: Nokia Pledges $4 Billion U.S. Investment in Trump Administration Partnership -- Update
data.description:
By Elias Schisgall


Nokia is investing an additional $4 billion in U.S.-based manufacturing and research and development in partnership with the Trump administration, as the Finnish telecommunications company looks to optimize its network infrastructure for artificial intelligence.

The company said Friday it plans to commit $3.5 billion to domestic research and development with a focus on AI-ready mobile, fixed access, IP, optical and data-center networking technologies centered at Nokia Bell Labs in New Jersey.

Another $500 million will go toward manufacturing and R&D across New Jersey, Texas and Pennsylvania.

Nokia is the latest company to increase its domestic AI spending under the Trump administration. Anthropic, OpenAI, Oracle, SoftBank, Meta and Apple have all announced significant commitments, some in the hundreds of billions of dollars, toward building AI infrastructure in the U.S. since January.

"Nokia's $4 billion investment is another Trump administration win for America," Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick said. "Their investment in manufacturing, packaging and R&D for optical chips means the most innovative technologies that power AI, data centers and critical national security applications will be developed and built here in the U.S.A."

The move also comes as Nokia looks to reposition itself to take advantage of the AI boom under Chief Executive Officer Justin Hotard, who began his role in April. The company has seen a spike in network infrastructure demand from AI and cloud customers, according to The Wall Street Journal.

The company said Wednesday that it would streamline its operations, reshuffle its leadership team and seek more joint partnerships on innovation as it refocuses on AI and cloud-computing growth, the Journal reported.

Nvidia took a $1 billion stake in Nokia late last month as part of a partnership on AI networking technology, according to the Journal.

Hotard said Friday that the new investment would bolster network infrastructure amid a flurry of investment in AI.

"Our expanded investment will help strengthen the nation's capacity to deliver greater security, productivity and prosperity through AI-optimized connectivity at scale, while advancing the newest research and innovation that will shape the future of networking for the years to come," Hotard said.


Write to Elias Schisgall at elias.schisgall@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

11-21-25 1127ET

US, global stocks set for weekly declines amid tech valuation concerns, mixed Fed messages
data.description:
NEW YORK (Reuters) -Wall Street stocks wobbled in choppy but range-bound trading on Friday as rising hopes of a December rate cut from the U.S. Federal Reserve were countered by persistent concerns over lofty tech valuations.

At last glance, the tech-laden Nasdaq was modestly lower, the S&P 500 was slightly higher and the blue-chip Dow was moving more decisively into positive territory.

Benchmark Treasury yields were lower, the dollar steady and bitcoin sharply lower.

Capping a tumultuous week, U.S. and world stocks appear set to end lower than last Friday's close, with the S&P 500 and the Nasdaq on track to record their largest weekly percentage loss since U.S. President Donald Trump rattled markets with his major tariff announcement in April.

Solid earnings from AI vanguards, most notably chipmaker Nvidia only momentarily quelled mounting concerns that AI-related tech stocks, which provided much of the muscle of the stock market's rally in recent months, are overpriced and could be due for a correction.

The Fed, deprived of official economic data during the recently ended government shutdown, at last got a glimpse of the state of the labor market on Thursday, which showed the unemployment rate unexpectedly ticking higher. 

As a result, financial markets are pricing in an increased likelihood of a third and final rate cut this year from the Fed. CME's FedWatch tool sets the odds at 73.3%, a significant bump from 39.1% on Thursday.

Messaging from monetary policymakers is mixed. New York Fed President John Williams said the Fed could still cut rates in the near term, while Dallas Fed President Lorie Logan called for them to be left on hold while the central bank assesses the effect of current rates on the economy.

"We continue to see a push and pull between good news and bad news," said Oliver Pursche, senior vice president at Wealthspire Advisors, in New York. "There are ongoing valuation concerns, there are concerns about the Fed's next move and the unemployment rate."

"The fourth quarter, which tends to be the strongest for stocks in terms of performance, has certainly seen a choppy start," Pursche added.

The Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 200.09 points, or 0.44%, to 45,952.35, the S&P 500 rose 3.54 points, or 0.04%, to 6,541.66 and the Nasdaq Composite fell 81.74 points, or 0.38%, to 21,993.43. 

European stocks slid on revived worries over stretched tech valuations.

MSCI's gauge of stocks across the globe  fell 4.07 points, or 0.42%, to 964.46.

The pan-European STOXX 600 index fell 0.44%, while Europe's broad FTSEurofirst 300 index fell 0.43%.

Emerging market stocks fell 2.81% to 1,333.04. MSCI's broadest index of Asia-Pacific shares outside Japan closed 2.83% lower at 684.69, while Japan's Nikkei fell 2.40% to 48,625.88.

The dollar looked set to register a weekly gain, and the yen found support as Japanese officials stepped up their verbal intervention to stem the currency's decline.

The dollar index, which measures the greenback against a basket of currencies including the yen and the euro, rose 0.12% to 100.28, with the euro down 0.22% at $1.1502.

Against the Japanese yen, the dollar weakened 0.73% to 156.33.

Cryptocurrencies sank to multi-month lows amid a broader flight from riskier assets. Bitcoin fell 5.08% to $82,789.98. Ethereum declined 6.44% to $2,692.87.

U.S. Treasury yields dipped as Fed rate cut bets rose.

The yield on benchmark U.S. 10-year notes fell 3.9 basis points to 4.065%, from 4.104% late on Thursday.

The 30-year bond yield fell 1.2 basis points to 4.7199% from 4.732% late on Thursday.

The 2-year note yield, which typically moves in step with interest rate expectations for the Fed, fell 5.3 basis points to 3.505%, from 3.558% late on Thursday.

Oil prices extended their decline for a third session as the U.S. pushed for a Russia-Ukraine peace deal.

U.S. crude fell 2.2% to $57.71 a barrel and Brent fell to $62.21 per barrel, down 1.81% on the day.

Gold pared earlier losses after dovish Fed comments boosted the probability of a December rate cut. Spot gold fell 0.31% to $4,064.19 an ounce. U.S. gold futures rose 0.35% to $4,070.90 an ounce.

(Reporting by Stephen Culp. Additional reporting by Elizabeth Howcroft. Editing by Mark Potter)

By Stephen Culp

data.title: Elon Musk's X down for thousands of US users, Downdetector shows
data.description:
(Reuters) -Elon Musk's X was down for thousands of users in the United States on Friday, according to Downdetector.com.

There were more than 19,500 reports of issues with the social media platform, as of 10:51 a.m. ET, according to Downdetector, which tracks outages by collating status reports from a number of sources.

(Reporting by Anhata Rooprai in Bengaluru; Editing by Shilpi Majumdar)

FUNDAMENTALco's Lyons, Bauer on Brand Value Creation
data.description:
Jenna Lyons, designer and partner at FUNDAMENTALco and former president of J.Crew, and Jonny Bauer, Blackstone's (BX) former global head of brand strategy and transformation, discuss their mission to rebrand private equity. Bauer tells Romaine Bostick and Katie Greifeld on "The Close" that their goal is to bring brand thinking much further upstream in the value-creation process and ask what a company could become next, aligning investors and management around that vision.
